section.title: The Trial, Crucifixion, and Burial
section.sectionRationale: The Gospel reaches its goal. Jesus is handed to Pilate, condemned in place of Barabbas, mocked as a king, and crucified at Golgotha. Darkness falls, and He cries out the anguish of forsakenness before He dies. At His death the temple veil is torn from top to bottom, and a Gentile centurion confesses Him the Son of God, the confession Mark has been driving toward from his first verse. Joseph of Arimathea lays Him in a tomb.
section.thesis: The King of the Jews reigns from a cross: condemned though innocent, dying in the place of a guilty man, forsaken by God so that His people need not be, and at His death the barrier to God is torn open and a Gentile soldier confesses Him the Son of God.
section.frameNote: Six beats: the trial before Pilate; the crowd's choice of Barabbas; the mocking by the soldiers; the crucifixion and mockery at the cross; the death of Jesus, the torn veil, and the centurion's confession; and the burial by Joseph of Arimathea.

units.label: The trial before Pilate
units.note: are You the King of the Jews

verses.text: Early in the morning, the chief priests, elders, scribes, and the whole Sanhedrin devised a plan. They bound Jesus, led Him away, and handed Him over to Pilate.
So Pilate questioned Him, “Are You the King of the Jews?” “You have said so,” Jesus replied.
And the chief priests began to accuse Him of many things.
Then Pilate questioned Him again, “Have You no answer? Look how many charges they are bringing against You!”
But to Pilate’s amazement, Jesus made no further reply.
Now it was Pilate’s custom at the feast to release to the people a prisoner of their choosing.
And a man named {{barabbas|Barabbas}} was imprisoned with the rebels who had committed murder during the insurrection.
So the crowd went up and began asking Pilate to keep his custom.
“Do you want me to release to you the King of the Jews?” Pilate asked.
For he knew it was out of envy that the chief priests had handed Jesus over.
But the chief priests stirred up the crowd to have him release Barabbas to them instead.
So Pilate asked them again, “What then do you want me to do with the One you call the King of the Jews?”
And they shouted back, “Crucify Him!”
“Why?” asked Pilate. “What evil has He done?” But they shouted all the louder, “Crucify Him!”
And wishing to satisfy the crowd, Pilate released Barabbas to them. But he had Jesus flogged, and handed Him over to be crucified.
Then the soldiers led Jesus away into the palace (that is, the Praetorium) and called the whole company together.
They dressed Him in a purple robe, twisted together a crown of thorns, and set it on His head.
And they began to salute Him: “Hail, King of the Jews!”
They kept striking His head with a staff and spitting on Him. And they knelt down and bowed before Him.
After they had mocked Him, they removed the purple robe and put His own clothes back on Him. Then they led Him out to crucify Him.

units.label: The crucifixion and the mockery
units.note: He saved others, but He cannot save Himself

verses.text: Now Simon of Cyrene, the father of Alexander and Rufus, was passing by on his way in from the country, and the soldiers forced him to carry the cross of Jesus.
They brought Jesus to a place called {{golgotha|Golgotha}}, which means The Place of the Skull.
There they offered Him wine mixed with myrrh, but He did not take it.
And they {{stauroo|crucified}} Him. They also divided His garments by casting lots to decide what each of them would take.
It was the third hour when they crucified Him.
And the charge inscribed against Him read: THE KING OF THE JEWS.
Along with Jesus, they crucified two robbers, one on His right and one on His left.
And those who passed by heaped abuse on Him, shaking their heads and saying, “Aha! You who are going to destroy the temple and rebuild it in three days,
come down from the cross and save Yourself!”
In the same way, the chief priests and scribes mocked Him among themselves, saying, “He saved others, but He cannot save Himself!
Let the Christ, the King of Israel, come down now from the cross, so that we may see and believe!” And even those who were crucified with Him berated Him.

units.label: The death of Jesus and the torn veil
units.note: truly this man was the Son of God

verses.text: From the sixth hour until the ninth hour darkness came over all the land.
At the ninth hour, Jesus cried out in a loud voice, “{{eloi|Eloi}}, Eloi, lema sabachthani?” which means, “My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me?”
When some of those standing nearby heard this, they said, “Behold, He is calling Elijah.”
And someone ran and filled a sponge with sour wine. He put it on a reed and held it up for Jesus to drink, saying, “Leave Him alone. Let us see if Elijah comes to take Him down.”
But Jesus let out a loud cry and breathed His last.
And the {{katapetasma|veil}} of the temple was torn in two from top to bottom.
When the centurion standing there in front of Jesus saw how He had breathed His last, he said, “Truly this man was the Son of God!”
And there were also women watching from a distance. Among them were Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James the younger and of Joses, and Salome.
These women had followed Jesus and ministered to Him while He was in Galilee, and there were many other women who had come up to Jerusalem with Him.

units.label: The burial by Joseph of Arimathea
units.note: laid in a tomb cut from the rock

verses.text: Now it was already evening. Since it was Preparation Day (that is, the day before the Sabbath),
Joseph of Arimathea, a prominent Council member who himself was waiting for the kingdom of God, boldly went to Pilate to ask for the body of Jesus.
Pilate was surprised to hear that Jesus was already dead, so he summoned the centurion to ask if this was so.
When Pilate had confirmed it with the centurion, he granted the body to Joseph.

So Joseph bought a linen cloth, took down the body of Jesus, wrapped it in the cloth, and placed it in a tomb that had been cut out of the rock. Then he rolled a stone against the entrance to the tomb.
Mary Magdalene and Mary the mother of Joseph saw where His body was placed.

barabbas.gloss: Barabbas, meaning son of the father, a murderer and insurrectionist. In a striking exchange the guilty son of the father goes free while the true Son of the Father dies in his place, a living picture of substitution: the innocent condemned so the guilty may be released.

golgotha.gloss: Golgotha, the Place of the Skull, the site outside Jerusalem where Jesus was crucified. Mark preserves the Aramaic name and translates it, grounding the event in a real place and time; here the King of the Jews is enthroned on a cross.

stauroo.gloss: To crucify, to execute on a cross. Crucifixion was Rome's most shameful and agonizing punishment, reserved for slaves and rebels. That the Son of God dies this death shows the depth of His humiliation and the seriousness of the sin He bears in His people's place.

eloi.gloss: My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me, the opening of Psalm 22 cried in Aramaic. Jesus voices the anguish of bearing God's judgment, truly forsaken so that His people never will be, yet praying a psalm that ends in vindication and worldwide worship.

katapetasma.gloss: The veil or curtain of the temple that barred the way into the Most Holy Place, God's symbolic dwelling. Torn from top to bottom at Jesus' death, by God's own hand, it signals that the way to God is now open through the death of His Son, and that the old temple order is ending.

basic.context: The leaders hand Jesus to Pilate, who asks if He is the King of the Jews. Jesus barely answers, and Pilate marvels. Following a feast custom, Pilate offers to release a prisoner; the crowd, stirred up by the priests, chooses the murderer Barabbas and demands that Jesus be crucified. Pilate, wanting to please the crowd, has Jesus flogged and handed over. Soldiers mock Him as a king with a purple robe and crown of thorns. At Golgotha they crucify Him between two robbers, cast lots for His clothes, and mock Him to save Himself. Darkness covers the land, and Jesus cries out, My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me, then dies. The temple veil tears in two, and a Gentile centurion says, Truly this man was the Son of God. Joseph of Arimathea buries Him in a rock tomb as the women watch.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the climax of Mark's Gospel and the answer to its opening line: this crucified man is the Son of God. Irony runs throughout: Jesus is mocked as King precisely when He is reigning from the cross, and told to save Himself when His refusal to do so is how He saves others. Barabbas dramatizes substitution. The torn veil and the centurion's confession interpret the death: access to God is opened, and the truth is confessed by a Gentile.
basic.originalLanguages: Barabbas, son of the father, goes free while the true Son dies. Jesus is stauroo, crucified, at golgotha, the Place of the Skull. He cries eloi, my God, in the words of Psalm 22, and at His death the katapetasma, the temple veil, is torn.
basic.typology: The innocent dying for the guilty fulfils the substitution pictured in the Passover and Isaiah 53. The dividing of garments and the cry of dereliction fulfil Psalm 22. The torn veil fulfils and ends the temple's separation, opening access to God.
basic.christ: Jesus is the King enthroned on a cross, the innocent substitute for the guilty, the forsaken Son who bears God's judgment, and the Son of God confessed at last. He saves others by refusing to save Himself.
basic.god: God works His salvation through the cross, tearing the veil by His own hand to open the way to Himself. God forsakes His Son in judgment so that sinners may be welcomed.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 22 supplies the cry and the divided garments. Isaiah 53 foretells the substitute numbered with transgressors. Hebrews 10 interprets the torn veil.
basic.oneStory: Everything the Gospel has built toward arrives here: the Son of God dies as King, in the place of the guilty, forsaken so His people need not be. The veil that kept sinners from God is torn open, and a Gentile confesses the truth Mark announced in his first verse. The story now waits, with the women, at a sealed tomb, for the third day.

context.literary: Chapter 15 is the crucifixion narrative, the summit of Mark. The centurion's confession, Truly this man was the Son of God, forms an inclusio with Mark 1:1, the Son of God, and with the Father's voice at the baptism and transfiguration; now a human being, and a Gentile, sees it at the cross. Mark tells the death with restraint, letting the events and Scripture allusions carry the meaning. The burial sets up the empty tomb.
context.historical: Pilate was the Roman governor with power of execution. The Passover-time prisoner release is otherwise attested only in the Gospels but fits Roman practice of clemency. Crucifixion was public, prolonged torture and utter shame. The temple veil separated the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place. Burial before sundown honoured the Sabbath and the law, Deuteronomy 21:23. Women were the faithful witnesses to death, burial, and resurrection.
context.canonical: The crucifixion is saturated with Psalm 22: the divided garments, verse 24 and Psalm 22:18; the mocking head-shaking, verse 29 and Psalm 22:7; and the cry of dereliction, verse 34 and Psalm 22:1. Jesus numbered with transgressors fulfils Isaiah 53:12, and the sour wine echoes Psalm 69:21. The torn veil is expounded in Hebrews 10:19-20 as the new and living way opened through Christ's flesh.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To present the crucifixion as the enthronement and saving death of the Son of God, to show through Barabbas, the mockery, the cry of forsakenness, the torn veil, and the centurion's confession that Jesus reigns and saves by dying in the place of sinners, opening the way to God, and to bring Mark's Gospel to its confessional climax.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes the once-for-all saving death of Christ, which cannot be repeated. It is not prescriptive in its events, but it calls for the response the centurion models: to look at the crucified Jesus and confess Him the Son of God. The faithful presence of the women and Joseph's courage are commended as examples of devotion.

debates.question: What does Jesus' cry, My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me, mean?

views.label: A real bearing of judgment and forsakenness
views.case: Jesus genuinely experiences the God-forsakenness due to sin, standing in the place of sinners under God's judgment; the darkness and the cry express the true cost of atonement, not mere despair.

views.label: A cry of faith quoting a psalm that ends in vindication
views.case: By praying the opening of Psalm 22, Jesus voices real anguish yet within a psalm that moves from forsakenness to deliverance and worldwide worship, so the cry is agony held within trust and hope.

debates.bearing: Both readings hold together: Jesus truly bears forsaking judgment for sin, and He does so as the faithful sufferer whose psalm, and whose death, will end in vindication and the salvation of the nations.

debates.question: What is the significance of the temple veil being torn in two?

views.label: The way to God is opened
views.case: The veil barred access to God's presence; torn from top to bottom by God's own act at Jesus' death, it declares that through the cross sinners now have direct access to God, as Hebrews 10 explains.

views.label: Judgment on the temple and the old order
views.case: The tearing signals the end of the temple system Jesus had pronounced doomed; with His death the true meeting place with God is no longer the building but the crucified and risen Christ.

debates.bearing: Both hold that the torn veil interprets the cross: access to God is opened through Christ, and the old temple order gives way to Him as the true and final meeting place with God.

study.originalLanguages: The scene turns on substitution: barabbas, whose name means son of the father, a guilty murderer, walks free while the true Son of the Father is condemned. Jesus is stauroo, crucified, the most shameful Roman death, at golgotha, the Place of the Skull. In the darkness He cries eloi, eloi, lema sabachthani, the opening of Psalm 22, bearing the forsakenness that sin deserves, yet praying a psalm that ends in triumph. At the moment He dies, the katapetasma, the great temple veil barring the way to God's presence, is torn from top to bottom, by God's own hand, and a Gentile centurion confesses what Mark announced in his very first line: truly this man was the Son of God.

typology.fulfillment: The innocent dying for the guilty, the substitution at the heart of the atonement, Isaiah 53:5-6 and 2 Corinthians 5:21.
The divided garments, the mocking, and the cry of forsakenness fulfilled in Jesus' crucifixion, Psalm 22:1, 7, 18.
The new and living way into God's presence opened through Christ's death, Hebrews 10:19-20.

study.christ: At the cross Jesus is revealed as King in the very act of being mocked as one: robed in purple, crowned with thorns, enthroned between two criminals under the placard, The King of the Jews. He saves others precisely by refusing to save Himself, staying on the cross to bear the judgment His mockers deserved. The guilty Barabbas set free is every sinner for whom the innocent Son dies. In the darkness Jesus drinks the cup to the dregs, forsaken by God so that His people never will be. And His death tears open the way to God and draws from a hardened Gentile soldier the confession the whole Gospel has aimed at: truly this man was the Son of God.
study.god: God is the unseen actor at the centre of the cross. It is God who tears the veil from top to bottom, from heaven's side, declaring the way to Himself now open through the death of His Son. It is God whose judgment on sin Jesus bears in the darkness and the cry of forsakenness, so that God can welcome sinners without compromising His justice. And it is God's long-laid plan, foretold in Psalm 22 and Isaiah 53, that unfolds in every detail, from the divided garments to the substitution of Barabbas. The cross is not the defeat of God's purpose but its accomplishment, the place where holy love saves the guilty.

crossReferences.note: My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me, the psalm Jesus cries from the cross, which moves to vindication and worldwide worship.
He was numbered with the transgressors, fulfilled as Jesus is crucified between two robbers.
God made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, the substitution Barabbas dramatizes.
We have confidence to enter the Holy Place by a new and living way through the curtain, that is, His flesh, the meaning of the torn veil.

study.oneStory: Everything Mark has built toward arrives at Golgotha. The Son of God is enthroned as King on a cross, dying in the place of the guilty, forsaken by God so that His people never will be. The murderer Barabbas walking free is the picture of the whole gospel: the innocent for the guilty. At His last breath the veil that fenced sinners out of God's presence is torn open from top to bottom, and a Gentile soldier confesses the truth of Mark's first line. The King is dead and buried, the women watching, the tomb sealed, and the whole creation now waits in the silence of the Sabbath for the third day.

quiz.q: What does the release of Barabbas in place of Jesus illustrate?

options.text: Substitution: the guilty man goes free while the innocent Son dies in his place.
options.note: Barabbas the murderer is released while Jesus is condemned, a living picture of substitution.

options.text: That Pilate was a fair and impartial judge.
options.note: Pilate acted to please the crowd against his own sense of Jesus' innocence; the point is substitution.

options.text: That the crowd made a tragic mistake in their choice.
options.note: The crowd did choose wrongly, but the deeper meaning is the innocent dying for the guilty.

options.text: The innocent taking the place of the guilty, as at the heart of the gospel.
options.note: Correct in substance, but it should name Barabbas specifically as the guilty man set free.

quiz.q: What is ironic about the mockery, He saved others, but He cannot save Himself?

options.text: He saves others precisely by refusing to save Himself, staying on the cross to bear their judgment.
options.note: The mockers speak truer than they know; His not coming down is how He saves, Mark 15:31.

options.text: He was actually unable to come down from the cross.
options.note: He could have come down; He chose to remain in order to save others.

options.text: The mockers accidentally spoke a deep truth about His saving death.
options.note: True, but it should state that His refusal to save Himself is the very means of saving others.

options.text: The mockery had no significance at all.
options.note: Mark records the irony deliberately: their taunt unwittingly states the gospel.

quiz.q: What did Jesus' cry, My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me, express?

options.text: His real bearing of God's judgment and forsakenness in the place of sinners, in the words of Psalm 22.
options.note: Mark 15:34; genuine forsakenness for sin, voiced through the psalm that ends in vindication.

options.text: That Jesus had lost His faith in God.
options.note: It is a prayer to God, quoting Scripture; anguish held within trust, not lost faith.

options.text: The depth of His suffering as He bore judgment for sin.
options.note: Correct in substance, but it should note He voices the real forsakenness in the words of Psalm 22.

options.text: A simple expression of physical pain.
options.note: The cry expresses a far deeper anguish than physical pain: God-forsakenness for sin.

quiz.q: What did the tearing of the temple veil at Jesus' death signify?

options.text: That the way to God is now opened through Christ's death, and the old temple order is ending.
options.note: Torn from top to bottom by God, the veil signals open access to God, Hebrews 10:19-20.

options.text: That the temple building had simply grown old and worn.
options.note: The tearing was a divine sign at the moment of Jesus' death, not natural wear.

options.text: That access to God's presence is now open through the cross.
options.note: True, but it should also include the judgment on the old temple order that the tearing signals.

options.text: That worship in the temple should now be more careful.
options.note: The torn veil points to open access through Christ, not to more careful temple ritual.

quiz.q: Who confessed the truth about Jesus at His death, and what did he say?

options.text: A Gentile centurion said, Truly this man was the Son of God, the confession Mark's Gospel aimed toward.
options.note: Mark 15:39 forms an inclusio with Mark 1:1, now confessed by a Gentile soldier.

options.text: The chief priests admitted He was the Messiah.
options.note: The priests mocked Him; it was the centurion who confessed Him the Son of God.

options.text: A Roman soldier acknowledged Jesus as the Son of God.
options.note: Correct, but it should note how this confession fulfils Mark's opening line about the Son of God.

options.text: The two robbers proclaimed Him King of Israel.
options.note: The robbers berated Him; the centurion made the great confession.

questions: Barabbas the guilty went free because Jesus the innocent took his place. How does it move you to see yourself in Barabbas, released because Christ died in your place?
Jesus was mocked as King even as He truly reigned from the cross. Where do you need eyes to see God's power and kingship in what looks like weakness or defeat?
Jesus saved others by not saving Himself. How does His refusal to come down from the cross reshape your understanding of love and sacrifice?
At the cross the way to God was torn open. How does the truth that you now have direct access to God through Christ change the way you approach Him?
A hardened Gentile soldier looked at the crucified Jesus and confessed Him the Son of God. As you look at the cross, what confession does it draw from you?

translationNotes.note: The BSB does not print a verse 28; the words some manuscripts add, He was numbered with the transgressors from Isaiah 53:12, are absent from the earliest witnesses here, so the numbering skips from 27 to 29, though the truth they state is fulfilled in the scene.
Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani is Aramaic, quoting Psalm 22:1; Mark preserves Jesus' actual words and translates them, My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me.
The veil torn from top to bottom indicates a divine act from heaven's side; it opens the way into God's presence, as Hebrews 10:19-20 explains, and marks the end of the old temple order.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 15:1-47. Footnote markers and line breaks were dropped when assembling verse text. Verse 28 is absent from the BSB main text and is noted; verses run 1 to 27 then 29 to 47. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. The Aramaic Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani is retained as in the BSB and glossed. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
